cards.title: Musculoskeletal Assessment — Systematic Approach
cards._legacyHtml:
1
Look — Posture, alignment, swelling, muscle wasting, skin changes, deformity (varus/valgus, kyphosis, scoliosis). Compare sides.
2
Feel — Temperature (↑ = active inflammation), tenderness (specific points vs diffuse), crepitus, effusion (patellar tap, cross-fluctuation), muscle tone
3
Move — Active then passive ROM. Pain on active only = muscular. Pain on passive = articular/capsular. End-feel: soft (effusion), firm (capsular tightness), hard (bone-on-bone). Record degrees.
4
Special tests — Specific to joint and suspected diagnosis (see below). Assess neurovascular status: sensation, pulses, reflexes distal to lesion.
5
Function — Can they perform their ADLs? Job-specific tasks? Sport movements? Use outcome tools (DASH, KOOS, Oswestry, VAS).
🟡 GALS screening
Gait-Arms-Legs-Spine. A 2-minute systematic screen: watch patient walk, assess shoulder abduction + hand function, knee squat, spinal flexion. Misses nothing significant. Ask: "Do you have any pain or stiffness in your muscles, joints or back?" — If yes, positive GALS → full regional exam required.

cards.title: Inflammatory vs Mechanical Pain — Differentiation
cards._legacyHtml:
FeatureInflammatory / ArthriticMechanical
Morning stiffness>60 minutes ★<30 minutes
Rest painYes — worse with restNo — improved with rest
Activity effectImproves with movementWorsens with movement
DistributionPolyarticular, symmetrical (RA), or axial (AS)Monoarticular, asymmetric
Systemic featuresFatigue, fever, weight loss, rashUsually absent
Night painCommonAbsent (unless positional)
Labs↑ CRP/ESR, RF, ANA, HLA-B27Normal
Naturopathic approachAnti-inflammatory protocol: omega-3, curcumin, elimination diet trialStructural: yoga, massage, exercise rehab, posture

cards.title: Low Back Pain — Naturopathic Management
cards._legacyHtml:
Red flags — refer urgently
Saddle anaesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction (cauda equina), night pain + weight loss (malignancy), fever + back pain (infection), age >50 new onset.
TypeFeaturesNaturopathic Tx
Non-specific LBP (90%)No neurological signs, no red flagsStay active ★, yoga (Iyengar) ★, massage, McKenzie exercises, heat, magnesium
Radiculopathy (disc)Radiating leg pain, dermatomal, ↑ with ValsalvaAnti-inflammatory: omega-3, curcumin, gentle extension exercises, refer if progressive
Spinal stenosisNeurogenic claudication, ↑ walking, ↓ sitting/leaning forwardCore strengthening, hydrotherapy, weight management
Sacroiliac jointPain below L5, FABER/FADIR positive, SI provocation testsProlotherapy (refer), SI belt, specific stabilisation exercises
🟡 Yoga for LBP — Grade A evidence
Iyengar yoga and general therapeutic yoga are Grade A evidence for chronic LBP — Cochrane review 2017. Effect size equivalent to or exceeding physiotherapy for pain and function at 6 months. Mechanism: improved core stability, flexibility, pain catastrophising reduction, HPA axis regulation. Prescribe: 60-minute class × 2/week for 12 weeks minimum. Avoid Bikram/hot yoga in acute phase.

cards.title: Osteoporosis — Assessment & Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Osteoporosis — T-score ≤ −2.5 (DEXA)
T-score −1.0 to −2.5 = Osteopenia. T-score ≤ −2.5 = Osteoporosis. Risk of fragility fracture increases 2x per SD decrease in BMD. Wrist, spine, and hip are primary fracture sites.
Risk FactorModifiableNon-Modifiable
MajorLow calcium/Vit D, smoking, alcohol, corticosteroids, low body weight, inactivityAge, female sex, menopause, family history, prior fragility fracture
Secondary causesCoeliac disease, IBD, thyrotoxicosis, hyperparathyroidism, T1DM, prolonged PPI use, anti-epileptics, aromatase inhibitors
InterventionDoseEvidenceNotes
Calcium ★1200mg/day total (food + supplement)★★★★Split doses — max 500mg absorbed per dose. Food sources preferred (dairy, sardines, tofu, leafy greens)
Vitamin D3 ★2000–4000 IU/day. Target 25(OH)D >75 nmol/L★★★★Must combine with calcium — D3 alone has less effect on fracture
Vitamin K2 (MK-7)100–200mcg/day★★★ GoodDirects calcium to bone (osteocalcin carboxylation), away from arteries. Essential with high-dose Ca+D3
Magnesium300–400mg/day★★★ GoodCofactor for Vit D activation and bone matrix proteins
Strontium ranelatePrescriptive in some countries★★★★ StrongReduces fracture risk 35–40%. Not available OTC everywhere
Resistance training ★2–3x/week, all major muscle groups★★★★ StrongMost potent stimulus for bone formation — mechanical loading activates osteoblasts
Isoflavones (post-menopausal)80–90mg/day soy isoflavones★★★ GoodOestrogenic effect on bone — reduces resorption
Collagen peptides10g/day★★★ GoodImproves bone mineral density + muscle mass in postmenopausal women (RCT)
🟡 FRAX Score
Use FRAX calculator (shef.ac.uk/FRAX) for every patient with osteopenia or risk factors. FRAX calculates 10-year probability of major osteoporotic fracture and hip fracture — guides treatment decisions. Treatment threshold varies by country. In India: consider bisphosphonates if 10-year hip fracture risk >3% or major fracture risk >20%, alongside naturopathic protocol. Always check secondary causes before starting treatment — untreated coeliac disease or hyperparathyroidism makes osteoporosis treatment ineffective.

cards.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis — Naturopathic Adjunct Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
RA — DMARDs Are Essential ★
Rheumatoid arthritis causes irreversible joint damage without disease-modifying treatment. DMARDs (methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, biologic agents) must be started early. Naturopathic interventions are adjuncts — they reduce inflammation, improve quality of life, and manage side effects but CANNOT replace DMARDs.
InterventionDoseEvidenceEffect
Fish oil (EPA+DHA) ★3–6g EPA/day★★★★ Strong — multiple RCTs and meta-analysesReduces morning stiffness, tender joint count, NSAID requirement. 3+ months for full effect
Anti-inflammatory dietMediterranean pattern — daily★★★★ StrongReduces DAS28 score, CRP, ESR. Eliminates: nightshades, refined carbs, red meat, alcohol
Boswellia serrata300–400mg TDS (65% boswellic acids)★★★ Good5-LOX inhibition — reduces leukotriene B4 (key RA inflammatory mediator)
Curcumin (with piperine)500–1000mg BD★★★ GoodNF-κB inhibition, TNF-α ↓, comparable to diclofenac in one RCT
ProbioticsLactobacillus acidophilus 10B CFU/day★★★ GoodModulates Th17/Treg balance — reduces autoimmune activity
Methotrexate supportFolate 5mg/day (prescribed alongside MTX)★★★★ EssentialReduces MTX side effects (nausea, mouth ulcers, liver toxicity) without reducing efficacy
Vit D34000 IU/day★★★ GoodImmune modulation, reduces autoimmunity. Most RA patients are Vit D deficient
🟡 Morning Stiffness Protocol
RA morning stiffness >1 hour is a key diagnostic feature AND activity marker. Naturopathic morning stiffness protocol: (1) Warm shower or bath immediately on waking (15–20 min — heat reduces joint viscosity). (2) Gentle range-of-motion exercises in warm water. (3) Fish oil taken with dinner the night before (acts overnight). (4) Anti-inflammatory breakfast (turmeric golden milk, omega-3 rich foods). (5) Paraffin wax bath for hands if severely affected. Target: stiffness <30 minutes with treatment.

cards.title: Gout — Naturopathic Assessment & Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Gout = Monosodium Urate Crystal Deposition
Serum uric acid >360 µmol/L (6 mg/dL) in women or >420 µmol/L (7 mg/dL) in men = hyperuricaemia. Gout occurs when urate crystals deposit in joints, soft tissue, and kidneys. 90% of gout is due to under-excretion of urate (not overproduction).
PhaseFeaturesManagement
Acute attackSudden onset excruciating pain, swollen hot red joint (1st MTP joint = podagra in 50%), peaks 12–24hrsRest, ice, elevation. Colchicine (prescriptive) or NSAIDs. Do NOT start allopurinol during acute attack.
IntercriticalAsymptomatic between attacks. Serum urate elevatedTarget urate <360 µmol/L with diet + possibly allopurinol. Prevent next attack.
Chronic tophaceousTophi (urate deposits), chronic joint damage, renal stones, nephrolithiasisUrate-lowering therapy (allopurinol/febuxostat) essential. Naturopathic adjunct.
Naturopathic InterventionProtocolEvidence
Tart cherry (Montmorency) ★240ml juice or 500mg extract BD★★★ Good — reduces urate 15–20%, reduces attack frequency 50%
Alkalisation protocolSodium bicarbonate 1/2 tsp in water BD (raises urine pH to 6.5–7.0 — urate more soluble)★★★ Good
Hydration ★3L water/day — dilutes serum urate, increases renal excretion★★★★ Essential — most important single intervention
Eliminate: fructose ★No sugary drinks, fruit juices, HFCS — fructose raises urate more than purine foods★★★★ Strong evidence
Eliminate: alcohol (beer > spirits)Beer contains purines + alcohol raises urate. Complete elimination during attacks★★★★ Strong
Reduce: red meat & organ meatLimit to 2x/week. Eliminate organ meats (liver, kidney, brain)★★★ Good
Vitamin C500–1500mg/day★★★ Good — reduces serum urate 0.5mg/dL (uricosuric effect)
Coffee (regular)3–4 cups/day if tolerated★★★ Good — reduces gout risk 40–60% (xanthine oxidase inhibition)
🟡 Gout Myth — Purine Foods
The traditional gout diet focused heavily on avoiding purine-rich foods (meat, fish, legumes). Modern evidence shows FRUCTOSE is a more powerful urate-raising agent than dietary purines. A can of cola raises serum urate more than a steak. Key changes: (1) Eliminate ALL sugary drinks and fruit juices. (2) Switch to water, coffee, and tart cherry juice. (3) Moderate meat — do not eliminate protein. Legumes (previously restricted) are now acceptable — their purines are less bioavailable. Dairy is actually protective against gout.

cards.title: Cervical Spine & Neck Pain — Assessment & Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Cervical Red Flags — Refer Urgently ★
Bilateral upper limb symptoms + gait disturbance = cervical myelopathy (cord compression) → urgent MRI + neurosurgery referral. Neck pain + fever + meningism = meningitis/discitis → emergency. Neck pain after trauma → cervical fracture until proven otherwise → immobilise, refer emergency.
ConditionKey FeaturesTestsNaturopathic Approach
Mechanical neck painLocalised, worse movement, no neurological symptomsClinical — range of motion assessmentHeat/cold, gentle ROM exercises, posture correction, massage, acupressure GB21/BL10
Cervical spondylosisAge-related, gradual onset, crepitus, restricted ROMX-ray (osteophytes, reduced disc height)Anti-inflammatory diet, omega-3, glucosamine, physiotherapy, hydrotherapy
Cervical radiculopathyArm pain/paraesthesia in dermatomal pattern, positive Spurling'sMRI cervical spine, nerve conductionTraction, anti-inflammatory Rx, acupressure, refer if not improving 6 weeks
Whiplash (WAD)Post-collision, delayed onset 12–24hrs, headache, restricted ROMNEXUS criteria (X-ray if positive)Early gentle mobilisation — NOT immobilisation (worse outcomes). Heat, TENS, massage from day 3
Tension-type headache from neckSuboccipital pain radiating to forehead/eyes, tender trigger pointsClinical — palpation of suboccipital musclesTrigger point therapy, Mg 400mg, posture, ergonomics, chin tucks exercise
E
Ergonomics Assessment Screen height: top of screen = eye level. Distance: arm's length. Chair: lumbar support, feet flat. Phone: never cradle between ear and shoulder. Sleeping: 1 pillow (neutral spine), not 2–3. Standing desks: alternate sitting/standing 30 min cycles. Text neck (head-forward posture) increases cervical spine load 4x — for every 1cm forward, load increases by 4kg.
C
Chin Tucks Exercise (DNF) Deep Neck Flexors (DNF) are weak in chronic neck pain. Chin tuck: retract chin horizontally (not down) — double chin position. Hold 5–10 sec, 10 reps, 3x/day. Strongest evidence-based exercise for mechanical neck pain. Strengthens longus colli and capitis — the neck's equivalent of the core. Progress to: supine chin tuck → sitting → resistance band.

cards.title: Low Back Pain — Assessment, Red Flags & Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
LBP Red Flags — NEVER Miss These
95% of LBP is benign mechanical. 5% has a serious underlying cause (tumour, fracture, infection, cauda equina). Missing these is a catastrophic clinical failure. Every LBP patient needs red flag screening at first presentation.
Red FlagConcernAction
Age >50 + no prior LBP episodeMalignancy, compression fracture (osteoporosis)XR lumbar spine. If cancer history: urgent MRI.
Bilateral leg weakness/numbness + bladder/bowel changeCauda equina syndrome — SURGICAL EMERGENCY999/108 immediately. MRI within hours. Do NOT delay.
Night pain waking from sleep (not positional)Malignancy, infectionESR, CRP, XR. Urgent referral if inflammatory markers ↑
Fever + LBPDiscitis/osteomyelitis (especially in IV drug users, recent surgery, diabetes)Emergency referral. MRI + blood cultures. IV antibiotics.
Unexplained weight loss + LBPMalignancyUrgent oncology workup. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis.
History of cancerMetastatic disease (prostate, breast, lung, kidney, thyroid most common)Urgent bone scan or MRI. Do NOT treat as mechanical LBP.
Saddle anaesthesia (perineal numbness)Cauda equina — EMERGENCYEmergency surgical referral immediately.
1
Mechanical LBP AssessmentMovement screen: forward flexion, extension, lateral flexion, rotation — identify movement direction preference. SLR (straight leg raise): <60° + radiating pain = disc herniation with nerve root involvement. Slump test: radicular pattern. FABER/FADIR for hip-origin referred pain. Neurological exam: dermatomal sensation, myotomal power (L4: tibialis anterior, L5: extensor hallucis longus, S1: gastrocnemius). Palpation: paravertebral muscle spasm, trigger points, facet joint tenderness.
2
Naturopathic LBP ProtocolAcute (<6 weeks): POLICE (Protect, Optimal Loading, Ice/heat, Compression, Elevation). Maintain activity (rest worsens outcomes ★★★). Heat therapy (moist heat 20 min) ★★★. Willow bark 240mg salicin/day ★★★ (comparable to diclofenac in RCT). Topical arnica/capsaicin ★★. Subacute/Chronic (>6 weeks): Exercise therapy ★★★ (most evidence-based treatment — any form). Yoga ★★★ (multiple RCTs — equal to physiotherapy). Acupuncture ★★★. Manual therapy ★★. Mindfulness-based stress reduction ★★★ (chronic LBP — addresses central sensitisation). Magnesium 400mg (muscle spasm). Anti-inflammatory diet.

cards.title: Knee Conditions — Assessment & Naturopathic Management
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionKey featuresSpecial testManagement
Osteoarthritis (OA) ★Age >45, morning stiffness <30 min, medial joint line pain, crepitus, Heberden/Bouchard nodes (hand OA)Medial/lateral joint line tenderness. X-ray: JSN, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosisExercise ★ (most evidence), weight loss (1kg off = 4kg off knee), glucosamine sulphate 1500mg, collagen hydrolysate, pool walking, hydrotherapy
Patellofemoral pain (PFPS)Anterior knee pain, worse stairs/squatting/prolonged sitting, "movie sign"Clarke's test (patellar grind), J-sign with extensionVMO strengthening (terminal extension), hip abductor strength, patellar taping, reduce hill running
Meniscal tearRotational injury or age-related (degenerative). Medial/lateral joint line pain, locking, giving way.McMurray test, Thessaly test (94% sensitive)Acute: RICE + early ROM. MRI for surgery decision. Exercise rehab often comparable to surgery for degenerative tears (RCT evidence).
ACL tearPivot/valgus injury, pop, immediate swelling (haemarthrosis), instabilityLachman test ★ (most sensitive), anterior drawer, pivot shiftSurgery vs rehab shared decision (depends on activity level, instability). Proprioception rehab regardless.
Patellar tendinopathyInferior patella pain, worse load (jumping, squatting). Jumper's knee in athletes.Palpation inferior pole. Royal London Hospital test.Heavy slow resistance ★ (isometric loading for pain relief, then eccentric decline squat). 12-week programme. Avoid rest alone.
🟡 Glucosamine sulphate — what the evidence actually says
Crystalline glucosamine sulphate (prescription-grade: Dona, Rottapharm) has the best evidence — GAIT trial subgroup and multiple European RCTs show ↓ pain, ↓ JSN progression in moderate-severe OA. Glucosamine hydrochloride (most OTC products) has much weaker evidence. Dose: 1500mg once daily (better than divided doses). Takes 4–8 weeks for effect. Combine with chondroitin sulphate 1200mg for synergistic effect.

cards.title: Hip Conditions — Assessment & Naturopathic
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionClinical FeaturesKey TestsNaturopathic Approach
Hip OsteoarthritisGroin/anterolateral hip pain, worse with activity and weight-bearing, morning stiffness <30 min, reduced IR and Flexion firstFADIR+ (flexion-adduction-IR = groin pain). FABER test (flexion-abduction-ER). XR: joint space narrowing, osteophytes. Thomas test (hip flexor contracture).Exercise ★★★ (most effective — aquatic, cycling). Weight loss per kg reduces hip joint force 3–4kg. Glucosamine 1500mg ★★, Vit D, omega-3. Hydrotherapy. Tai chi ★★★ for OA hip pain.
Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome (GTPS)Lateral hip pain, worse lying on affected side, ascending stairs, cross-leg sitting. Tender over greater trochanter. Previously called "bursitis" but primarily tendinopathy.Palpation over greater trochanter. FABER test ± positive. Single-leg squat provokes pain. Hip abductor weakness.Load management (avoid aggravating positions). Isometric gluteal exercises (pain-free muscle activation). Eccentric gluteal loading progression. Physiotherapy ★★★. Avoid: sleeping on affected side, pigeon stretch (compresses tendon), foam rolling over GT.
Femoroacetabular Impingement (FAI)Young active patients. Groin pain with deep hip flexion. Cam deformity (femoral head non-spherical). Pincer deformity (excessive acetabular coverage).FADIR test ★ (flexion 90° + adduction + IR → groin pain). Scour test. CT or MRI arthrography for definitive assessment. XR: pistol grip deformity (cam), crossover sign (pincer).Activity modification. Physiotherapy: hip strengthening + movement pattern retraining. Avoid: deep squats, hip flexion end-range. Intra-articular injection (corticosteroid) diagnostic + therapeutic. Surgery if conservative management fails >6 months.
Hip Flexor Tightness & Psoas SyndromeAnterior hip/groin pain. Often in: office workers, cyclists, runners. Thomas test positive. Lumbar hyperlordosis secondary to tight psoas.Thomas test: patient supine, draws one knee to chest — contralateral leg rises off table = hip flexor tightness. Modified Thomas: degree of hip extension measured.Hip flexor stretching (lunge stretch, Thomas stretch — 3×30sec, 3×/day). Yoga: Anjaneyasana, Bhujangasana, Supta Virasana (hero reclining). Foam rolling for iliopsoas and rectus femoris. Core stability strengthening (co-activation of psoas + deep abdominals).
🟡 Hip Yoga Protocol
Top 5 yoga postures for hip health: (1) Baddha Konasana (butterfly) — inner groin, adductors. (2) Eka Pada Raja Kapotasana (pigeon) — piriformis, external rotators — CONTRAINDICATED in FAI and GTPS (compressive load on tendon and acetabular rim). (3) Virasana (hero pose) — hip flexors, quads. (4) Anjaneyasana (crescent lunge) — psoas, hip flexors. (5) Supta Kapotasana (supine pigeon/figure 4) — external rotators, piriformis — safer alternative to pigeon for FAI. Always assess: does this patient have FAI or GTPS before prescribing pigeon pose — in these conditions it is specifically contraindicated.

cards.title: Ankle & Foot Conditions
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionClinical FeaturesDiagnosisNaturopathic Management
Plantar FasciitisHeel pain worst with first steps in morning (post-sleep stiffness). Tender at medial calcaneal tubercle (origin of plantar fascia). Risk factors: BMI↑, sudden activity increase, flat feet or high arch, poor footwear.Clinical diagnosis (history + palpation usually sufficient). USS confirms thickened fascia >4mm. XR: may show heel spur (epiphenomenon — not cause).IASTM (instrument-assisted soft tissue mobilisation) ★★★ to plantar fascia. Plantar fascia stretching: cross-leg heel stretch + towel stretching (before standing, 3×30sec). Intrinsic foot strengthening: short foot exercise, towel scrunches. Orthotic insoles ★★ (offloading). Night splint (maintains dorsiflexion during sleep, preventing morning contracture). Achilles stretching (tight Achilles → ↑ plantar fascia load). Weight loss ★★★.
Ankle Sprain — GradingGrade 1: stretch, no tear. Grade 2: partial tear, moderate swelling. Grade 3: complete tear, significant instability.Ottawa Ankle Rules: XR if: bone tenderness at posterior edge/tip of fibula or tibia, or inability to weight-bear 4 steps. ATFL most commonly injured.POLICE protocol: Protection, Optimal Loading (early movement), Ice (20 min, 4×/day × 48hrs), Compression, Elevation. Early mobilisation ★★★ — NOT prolonged immobilisation. Proprioception rehab: single-leg balance 30 sec × 3 sets. Balance board. For Grade 3: refer orthopaedics. Arnica 30C topical ★★ (reduces bruising). Bromelain 500mg TID between meals ★★ (reduces post-sprain oedema and pain).
Morton's NeuromaBurning, tingling, numbness in 3rd-4th toe web space. "Walking on a pebble" sensation. Worse in tight shoes, high heels. Click felt on lateral squeeze of forefoot (Mulder's click).Mulder's test ★ (lateral squeeze + direct web space pressure). USS diagnostic (hypoechoic fusiform mass in web space). MRI if uncertain.Wide toe-box shoes (most important). Metatarsal dome orthotic (offloads web space). Avoid: heels, narrow shoes. Nerve mobilisation (neural flossing) ★★. Steroid injection (conventional) if conservative fails. Alcohol sclerosing injection. Surgery last resort.
Hallux Valgus (bunion)Medial deviation of great toe MTP joint. Prominent bony medial eminence. Secondary hammer toe, metatarsalgia.Clinical. XR: HVA angle (>15° abnormal, >40° severe). IMA angle between M1 and M2.Conservative: wide toe box footwear, toe separator, orthotics (realign load). Intrinsic foot strengthening (adductor hallucis strengthening). Bunion splints (may slow progression — no reversal). Yoga: toe spreading exercises, toe flexor/extensor balance. Referral orthopaedics if: intractable pain, HVA >40°, failed conservative >12 months.

cards.title: Shoulder Conditions — Systematic Approach
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionKey featuresSpecial testTreatment
Rotator cuff tendinopathy (impingement)Painful arc 60–120°, worse overhead, anterior shoulder pain, worse at nightHawkins-Kennedy ★ (most sensitive), Neer's, Empty can (supraspinatus)Rotator cuff strengthening (external rotation ★), scapular stabilisation, eccentric loading. Avoid injections unless failed 6 weeks physio.
Rotator cuff tearWeakness + pain, often trauma or sudden onset. Lag signs positive.Drop arm test (full thickness), Hornblower's test (teres minor), Belly-press (subscapularis)Partial: physio 3–6 months. Full thickness in young/active: surgical referral. MRI for confirmation.
Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder)Global restriction ALL movements (unlike impingement — only painful arc). Phases: freezing (pain) → frozen (stiffness) → thawing.Capsular pattern: external rotation > abduction > internal rotation restrictedNSAIDs + intra-articular steroid (freezing phase). Physiotherapy + hydrodilatation. Self-resolves in 1–3 years. Pendulum exercises maintain range.
AC joint painSuperior shoulder pain, tender AC joint, worse cross-body adductionAC joint palpation, cross-body adduction test, O'Brien's test (SLAP)Rest, ice, NSAIDs. Steroid injection if persistent. Surgery rare.
🟡 Impingement vs tear — the clinical distinction
Both cause painful arc. Key distinguishing feature: true WEAKNESS = full-thickness tear until proven otherwise. Test with resisted abduction (supraspinatus — empty can/zero-degree), external rotation (infraspinatus/teres minor), and internal rotation (subscapularis — belly press/lift-off). Weakness in any of these = MRI to rule out tear before embarking on prolonged conservative management.

cards.title: Sports Injury — POLICE Protocol & Rehabilitation Stages
cards._legacyHtml:
POLICE (replaces RICE)
Optimal Loading is now preferred over Rest — controlled movement promotes healing and prevents deconditioning.
LetterInterventionHow to apply
P — ProtectionLimit harmful movementSplint/brace only if needed. Avoid complete immobilisation.
OL — Optimal LoadingEarly controlled movement ★Pain-free ROM exercises within 24–48 hours. Promotes collagen alignment.
I — IceCryotherapy (first 48–72h)10–20 min, 3–4× daily. Reduces pain + spasm. Evidence mixed for swelling.
C — CompressionElastic bandagingReduces oedema. Check for neurovascular compromise distally.
E — ElevationAbove heart levelReduces hydrostatic pressure + oedema.
PhaseTimelineGoalNaturopathic support
Acute (inflammatory)0–72 hoursControl swelling, pain managementBromelain 500mg TID, arnica topical, turmeric, ice
Proliferative (repair)3 days – 3 weeksCollagen synthesis, restore ROMVitamin C 1–2g/day ★ (collagen synthesis), zinc, silica
Remodelling3 weeks – 12 monthsStrength, proprioception, return to sportProgressive loading, protein 1.6–2.2g/kg, omega-3, yoga
🟡 Bromelain for acute injury
Bromelain (pineapple enzyme) 500–1000mg TID between meals reduces post-traumatic oedema, pain, and bruising as effectively as diclofenac in RCTs. Must be taken away from food (otherwise acts as digestive enzyme, not anti-inflammatory). Combine with curcumin and quercetin for synergistic anti-inflammatory effect. Avoid if on anticoagulants (antiplatelet activity).

cards.title: Nutritional Support for Joints & Connective Tissue
cards._legacyHtml:
NutrientRoleDoseEvidence
Glucosamine sulphateProteoglycan synthesis, cartilage matrix1500mg/dayGrade B for knee OA (sulfate > hydrochloride)
Chondroitin sulphateCartilage hydration, anti-catabolic800–1200mg/dayGrade B OA; combined with glucosamine
Type II collagen (undenatured)Oral tolerance → ↓ autoimmune cartilage destruction40µg/day (UC-II)Grade B RA adjunct, OA
Omega-3 (EPA+DHA)↓ Leukotriene B4, ↓ TNF-α, ↓ IL-1β2–4g/day EPA+DHAGrade A RA ★, Grade B OA
Vitamin D3Muscle function, pain modulation, anti-inflammatory2000–4000 IU/dayGrade B musculoskeletal pain, falls prevention
Magnesium (glycinate/malate)Muscle relaxation, nerve conduction, ATP300–400mg/dayGrade B muscle cramps, fibromyalgia
Boswellia serrata5-LOX inhibition → ↓ leukotrienes, anti-inflammatory300–400mg 3× (65% boswellic acids)Grade A OA knee ★, IBD adjunct
🟡 Boswellia — fastest onset herbal anti-inflammatory
Boswellia works within 1–2 weeks (vs 4–8 weeks for omega-3). Mechanism: selectively inhibits 5-lipoxygenase (5-LOX) → ↓ leukotrienes (not prostaglandins like NSAIDs) → anti-inflammatory without GI ulceration risk. AKBA (acetyl-11-keto-β-boswellic acid) is the most active component. Aflapin (patented form) shows superior bioavailability. Pair with curcumin for additive effect via complementary pathways.

cards.title: Fibromyalgia — Comprehensive Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Fibromyalgia — Central Sensitisation Syndrome
Fibromyalgia is not a psychological diagnosis — it is a disorder of central pain processing. Neuroimaging shows genuine amplified pain signal processing in the central nervous system. The 2016 ACR criteria: Widespread Pain Index ≥7 + Symptom Severity Scale ≥5, for >3 months. No longer requires tender point count.
InterventionEvidenceProtocolNotes
Aerobic exercise★★★ Most consistent evidence — reduces pain 30%, fatigue 25%, improves function. Aquatic exercise ★★★ (heated pool — unloaded, gentle, joint-friendly)Start very low (5–10 min walk). Increase by 10% per week. Goal: 150 min moderate aerobic/week over 3–6 months. Never push through post-exertional pain flare.Graded exercise therapy: most important non-pharmacological intervention. Resistance training also effective ★★.
Sleep priority★★★ Poor sleep → central sensitisation → more pain. Treating insomnia improves pain in FM ★★★ (fibromyalgia is as much a sleep disorder as a pain disorder)CBT-I for insomnia (first-line). Magnesium glycinate 400mg + malate 600mg. Sleep hygiene. Low-dose amitriptyline 10–25mg (conventional — improves sleep architecture and reduces FM pain).Without treating sleep, FM is extremely difficult to manage. Always prioritise sleep assessment and treatment.
SAMe (S-adenosyl methionine)★★★ Multiple RCTs: SAMe 400–800mg/day reduces FM pain, fatigue, depression, morning stiffnessSAMe 200mg BD increasing to 400mg BD over 4 weeks. Takes 4–8 weeks for effect. Antidepressant + analgesic + methylation donor.Avoid in: bipolar disorder (can trigger mania). Store in cool, dark place (unstable molecule). Expensive but strong evidence in FM specifically.
Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR)★★★ Cochrane: MBSR reduces pain catastrophising, improves pain acceptance and QoL in fibromyalgia8-week MBSR programme ★★★. Yoga Nidra daily. Breathwork (reduces sympathetic amplification of pain). Pain Neuroscience Education (PNE) ★★★ — explaining central sensitisation to patients reduces pain and catastrophising.Pain Neuroscience Education is one of the most powerful FM interventions — patients who understand WHY they have pain AND that it is not dangerous respond much better to all other treatments.

cards.title: Tendinopathy — Eccentric Loading & Nutrition
cards._legacyHtml:
Tendinopathy — The Continuum Model
Cook and Purdam's tendon continuum: Reactive tendinopathy → Tendon disrepair → Degenerative tendinopathy. Stage determines treatment. Reactive tendinopathy: acute load management + isometrics. Disrepair/degenerative: progressive loading is THE most evidence-based treatment. Complete rest = tendon atrophy → worse prognosis.
TendonClinical FeaturesExercise Protocol
Achilles tendinopathyPosterior heel pain, morning stiffness (post-sleep stiffness that warms up with activity — classic). Tender on palpation mid-body or insertion. Runners, jumping sports, sudden increase in load.Alfredson protocol ★★★: eccentric calf raises 3×15 reps with straight knee + 3×15 with bent knee, twice daily × 12 weeks. Load progression: start BW, add weight as pain allows. HEAVY slow resistance (Beyer RCT) — as effective as Alfredson, better tolerated.
Patella tendinopathyAnterior knee pain at patella inferior pole. Worse: jumping, landing, stairs, sitting with bent knee. Athletes (volleyball, basketball — "jumper's knee").Isometric quad contractions 4×45sec → progress to single leg press heavy slow resistance. Spanish squat (horizontal force vector). Decline squat programme ★★★. Avoid: stretching painful tendon, deep squat early stages.
Rotator cuff tendinopathyAnterior/lateral shoulder pain, worse overhead activity. Common in: overhead athletes, painters, elderly (degenerative). Painful arc 60–120°.Rotator cuff isometrics (pain-free angles). Progress to: external rotation resistance band, side-lying ER, empty can → full can, and Y-T-W-L scapular exercises. Thoracic mobility essential.
1
Nutritional Support for Tendon Healing Collagen peptides (Fortigel, TENDOFORTE) 10–15g + 50mg Vit C 30–60 min before loading exercise ★★★ — Shaw et al. RCT: doubles tendon collagen synthesis. Collagen peptides provide hydroxyproline and proline — building blocks loaded specifically into tendon when mechanical stimulus is applied within 1 hr of ingestion. Vitamin C 500mg/day ★★★ — essential cofactor for prolyl hydroxylase (crosslinks collagen triple helix). Without adequate Vit C, structurally weak collagen forms. Glycine 3–5g/day (rate-limiting amino acid for collagen — often deficient in muscle-protein-focused diets). Vitamin D 3000–4000 IU (VDR expressed in tendon cells — deficiency → slower tendon healing). Omega-3 3g/day (reduces neurogenic inflammation in tendinopathy).

cards.title: Low Back Pain — Naturopathic Management
cards._legacyHtml:
Red flags — refer urgently
Saddle anaesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction (cauda equina), night pain + weight loss (malignancy), fever + back pain (infection), age >50 new onset.
TypeFeaturesNaturopathic Tx
Non-specific LBP (90%)No neurological signs, no red flagsStay active ★, yoga (Iyengar) ★, massage, McKenzie exercises, heat, magnesium
Radiculopathy (disc)Radiating leg pain, dermatomal, ↑ with ValsalvaAnti-inflammatory: omega-3, curcumin, gentle extension exercises, refer if progressive
Spinal stenosisNeurogenic claudication, ↑ walking, ↓ sitting/leaning forwardCore strengthening, hydrotherapy, weight management
Sacroiliac jointPain below L5, FABER/FADIR positive, SI provocation testsProlotherapy (refer), SI belt, specific stabilisation exercises
🟡 Yoga for LBP — Grade A evidence
Iyengar yoga and general therapeutic yoga are Grade A evidence for chronic LBP — Cochrane review 2017. Effect size equivalent to or exceeding physiotherapy for pain and function at 6 months. Mechanism: improved core stability, flexibility, pain catastrophising reduction, HPA axis regulation. Prescribe: 60-minute class × 2/week for 12 weeks minimum. Avoid Bikram/hot yoga in acute phase.

cards.title: Cervical Spine & Neck Pain — Assessment & Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Cervical Red Flags — Refer Urgently ★
Bilateral upper limb symptoms + gait disturbance = cervical myelopathy (cord compression) → urgent MRI + neurosurgery referral. Neck pain + fever + meningism = meningitis/discitis → emergency. Neck pain after trauma → cervical fracture until proven otherwise → immobilise, refer emergency.
ConditionKey FeaturesTestsNaturopathic Approach
Mechanical neck painLocalised, worse movement, no neurological symptomsClinical — range of motion assessmentHeat/cold, gentle ROM exercises, posture correction, massage, acupressure GB21/BL10
Cervical spondylosisAge-related, gradual onset, crepitus, restricted ROMX-ray (osteophytes, reduced disc height)Anti-inflammatory diet, omega-3, glucosamine, physiotherapy, hydrotherapy
Cervical radiculopathyArm pain/paraesthesia in dermatomal pattern, positive Spurling'sMRI cervical spine, nerve conductionTraction, anti-inflammatory Rx, acupressure, refer if not improving 6 weeks
Whiplash (WAD)Post-collision, delayed onset 12–24hrs, headache, restricted ROMNEXUS criteria (X-ray if positive)Early gentle mobilisation — NOT immobilisation (worse outcomes). Heat, TENS, massage from day 3
Tension-type headache from neckSuboccipital pain radiating to forehead/eyes, tender trigger pointsClinical — palpation of suboccipital musclesTrigger point therapy, Mg 400mg, posture, ergonomics, chin tucks exercise
E
Ergonomics Assessment Screen height: top of screen = eye level. Distance: arm's length. Chair: lumbar support, feet flat. Phone: never cradle between ear and shoulder. Sleeping: 1 pillow (neutral spine), not 2–3. Standing desks: alternate sitting/standing 30 min cycles. Text neck (head-forward posture) increases cervical spine load 4x — for every 1cm forward, load increases by 4kg.
C
Chin Tucks Exercise (DNF) Deep Neck Flexors (DNF) are weak in chronic neck pain. Chin tuck: retract chin horizontally (not down) — double chin position. Hold 5–10 sec, 10 reps, 3x/day. Strongest evidence-based exercise for mechanical neck pain. Strengthens longus colli and capitis — the neck's equivalent of the core. Progress to: supine chin tuck → sitting → resistance band.

cards.title: Ankle & Foot Conditions
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionClinical FeaturesDiagnosisNaturopathic Management
Plantar FasciitisHeel pain worst with first steps in morning (post-sleep stiffness). Tender at medial calcaneal tubercle (origin of plantar fascia). Risk factors: BMI↑, sudden activity increase, flat feet or high arch, poor footwear.Clinical diagnosis (history + palpation usually sufficient). USS confirms thickened fascia >4mm. XR: may show heel spur (epiphenomenon — not cause).IASTM (instrument-assisted soft tissue mobilisation) ★★★ to plantar fascia. Plantar fascia stretching: cross-leg heel stretch + towel stretching (before standing, 3×30sec). Intrinsic foot strengthening: short foot exercise, towel scrunches. Orthotic insoles ★★ (offloading). Night splint (maintains dorsiflexion during sleep, preventing morning contracture). Achilles stretching (tight Achilles → ↑ plantar fascia load). Weight loss ★★★.
Ankle Sprain — GradingGrade 1: stretch, no tear. Grade 2: partial tear, moderate swelling. Grade 3: complete tear, significant instability.Ottawa Ankle Rules: XR if: bone tenderness at posterior edge/tip of fibula or tibia, or inability to weight-bear 4 steps. ATFL most commonly injured.POLICE protocol: Protection, Optimal Loading (early movement), Ice (20 min, 4×/day × 48hrs), Compression, Elevation. Early mobilisation ★★★ — NOT prolonged immobilisation. Proprioception rehab: single-leg balance 30 sec × 3 sets. Balance board. For Grade 3: refer orthopaedics. Arnica 30C topical ★★ (reduces bruising). Bromelain 500mg TID between meals ★★ (reduces post-sprain oedema and pain).
Morton's NeuromaBurning, tingling, numbness in 3rd-4th toe web space. "Walking on a pebble" sensation. Worse in tight shoes, high heels. Click felt on lateral squeeze of forefoot (Mulder's click).Mulder's test ★ (lateral squeeze + direct web space pressure). USS diagnostic (hypoechoic fusiform mass in web space). MRI if uncertain.Wide toe-box shoes (most important). Metatarsal dome orthotic (offloads web space). Avoid: heels, narrow shoes. Nerve mobilisation (neural flossing) ★★. Steroid injection (conventional) if conservative fails. Alcohol sclerosing injection. Surgery last resort.
Hallux Valgus (bunion)Medial deviation of great toe MTP joint. Prominent bony medial eminence. Secondary hammer toe, metatarsalgia.Clinical. XR: HVA angle (>15° abnormal, >40° severe). IMA angle between M1 and M2.Conservative: wide toe box footwear, toe separator, orthotics (realign load). Intrinsic foot strengthening (adductor hallucis strengthening). Bunion splints (may slow progression — no reversal). Yoga: toe spreading exercises, toe flexor/extensor balance. Referral orthopaedics if: intractable pain, HVA >40°, failed conservative >12 months.

cards.title: Shoulder Conditions — Systematic Approach
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionKey featuresSpecial testTreatment
Rotator cuff tendinopathy (impingement)Painful arc 60–120°, worse overhead, anterior shoulder pain, worse at nightHawkins-Kennedy ★ (most sensitive), Neer's, Empty can (supraspinatus)Rotator cuff strengthening (external rotation ★), scapular stabilisation, eccentric loading. Avoid injections unless failed 6 weeks physio.
Rotator cuff tearWeakness + pain, often trauma or sudden onset. Lag signs positive.Drop arm test (full thickness), Hornblower's test (teres minor), Belly-press (subscapularis)Partial: physio 3–6 months. Full thickness in young/active: surgical referral. MRI for confirmation.
Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder)Global restriction ALL movements (unlike impingement — only painful arc). Phases: freezing (pain) → frozen (stiffness) → thawing.Capsular pattern: external rotation > abduction > internal rotation restrictedNSAIDs + intra-articular steroid (freezing phase). Physiotherapy + hydrodilatation. Self-resolves in 1–3 years. Pendulum exercises maintain range.
AC joint painSuperior shoulder pain, tender AC joint, worse cross-body adductionAC joint palpation, cross-body adduction test, O'Brien's test (SLAP)Rest, ice, NSAIDs. Steroid injection if persistent. Surgery rare.
🟡 Impingement vs tear — the clinical distinction
Both cause painful arc. Key distinguishing feature: true WEAKNESS = full-thickness tear until proven otherwise. Test with resisted abduction (supraspinatus — empty can/zero-degree), external rotation (infraspinatus/teres minor), and internal rotation (subscapularis — belly press/lift-off). Weakness in any of these = MRI to rule out tear before embarking on prolonged conservative management.

cards.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis — Naturopathic Adjunct Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
RA — DMARDs Are Essential ★
Rheumatoid arthritis causes irreversible joint damage without disease-modifying treatment. DMARDs (methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, biologic agents) must be started early. Naturopathic interventions are adjuncts — they reduce inflammation, improve quality of life, and manage side effects but CANNOT replace DMARDs.
InterventionDoseEvidenceEffect
Fish oil (EPA+DHA) ★3–6g EPA/day★★★★ Strong — multiple RCTs and meta-analysesReduces morning stiffness, tender joint count, NSAID requirement. 3+ months for full effect
Anti-inflammatory dietMediterranean pattern — daily★★★★ StrongReduces DAS28 score, CRP, ESR. Eliminates: nightshades, refined carbs, red meat, alcohol
Boswellia serrata300–400mg TDS (65% boswellic acids)★★★ Good5-LOX inhibition — reduces leukotriene B4 (key RA inflammatory mediator)
Curcumin (with piperine)500–1000mg BD★★★ GoodNF-κB inhibition, TNF-α ↓, comparable to diclofenac in one RCT
ProbioticsLactobacillus acidophilus 10B CFU/day★★★ GoodModulates Th17/Treg balance — reduces autoimmune activity
Methotrexate supportFolate 5mg/day (prescribed alongside MTX)★★★★ EssentialReduces MTX side effects (nausea, mouth ulcers, liver toxicity) without reducing efficacy
Vit D34000 IU/day★★★ GoodImmune modulation, reduces autoimmunity. Most RA patients are Vit D deficient
🟡 Morning Stiffness Protocol
RA morning stiffness >1 hour is a key diagnostic feature AND activity marker. Naturopathic morning stiffness protocol: (1) Warm shower or bath immediately on waking (15–20 min — heat reduces joint viscosity). (2) Gentle range-of-motion exercises in warm water. (3) Fish oil taken with dinner the night before (acts overnight). (4) Anti-inflammatory breakfast (turmeric golden milk, omega-3 rich foods). (5) Paraffin wax bath for hands if severely affected. Target: stiffness <30 minutes with treatment.

cards.title: Gout — Naturopathic Assessment & Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Gout = Monosodium Urate Crystal Deposition
Serum uric acid >360 µmol/L (6 mg/dL) in women or >420 µmol/L (7 mg/dL) in men = hyperuricaemia. Gout occurs when urate crystals deposit in joints, soft tissue, and kidneys. 90% of gout is due to under-excretion of urate (not overproduction).
PhaseFeaturesManagement
Acute attackSudden onset excruciating pain, swollen hot red joint (1st MTP joint = podagra in 50%), peaks 12–24hrsRest, ice, elevation. Colchicine (prescriptive) or NSAIDs. Do NOT start allopurinol during acute attack.
IntercriticalAsymptomatic between attacks. Serum urate elevatedTarget urate <360 µmol/L with diet + possibly allopurinol. Prevent next attack.
Chronic tophaceousTophi (urate deposits), chronic joint damage, renal stones, nephrolithiasisUrate-lowering therapy (allopurinol/febuxostat) essential. Naturopathic adjunct.
Naturopathic InterventionProtocolEvidence
Tart cherry (Montmorency) ★240ml juice or 500mg extract BD★★★ Good — reduces urate 15–20%, reduces attack frequency 50%
Alkalisation protocolSodium bicarbonate 1/2 tsp in water BD (raises urine pH to 6.5–7.0 — urate more soluble)★★★ Good
Hydration ★3L water/day — dilutes serum urate, increases renal excretion★★★★ Essential — most important single intervention
Eliminate: fructose ★No sugary drinks, fruit juices, HFCS — fructose raises urate more than purine foods★★★★ Strong evidence
Eliminate: alcohol (beer > spirits)Beer contains purines + alcohol raises urate. Complete elimination during attacks★★★★ Strong
Reduce: red meat & organ meatLimit to 2x/week. Eliminate organ meats (liver, kidney, brain)★★★ Good
Vitamin C500–1500mg/day★★★ Good — reduces serum urate 0.5mg/dL (uricosuric effect)
Coffee (regular)3–4 cups/day if tolerated★★★ Good — reduces gout risk 40–60% (xanthine oxidase inhibition)
🟡 Gout Myth — Purine Foods
The traditional gout diet focused heavily on avoiding purine-rich foods (meat, fish, legumes). Modern evidence shows FRUCTOSE is a more powerful urate-raising agent than dietary purines. A can of cola raises serum urate more than a steak. Key changes: (1) Eliminate ALL sugary drinks and fruit juices. (2) Switch to water, coffee, and tart cherry juice. (3) Moderate meat — do not eliminate protein. Legumes (previously restricted) are now acceptable — their purines are less bioavailable. Dairy is actually protective against gout.

cards.title: Hip Conditions — Assessment & Naturopathic
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionClinical FeaturesKey TestsNaturopathic Approach
Hip OsteoarthritisGroin/anterolateral hip pain, worse with activity and weight-bearing, morning stiffness <30 min, reduced IR and Flexion firstFADIR+ (flexion-adduction-IR = groin pain). FABER test (flexion-abduction-ER). XR: joint space narrowing, osteophytes. Thomas test (hip flexor contracture).Exercise ★★★ (most effective — aquatic, cycling). Weight loss per kg reduces hip joint force 3–4kg. Glucosamine 1500mg ★★, Vit D, omega-3. Hydrotherapy. Tai chi ★★★ for OA hip pain.
Greater Trochanteric Pain Syndrome (GTPS)Lateral hip pain, worse lying on affected side, ascending stairs, cross-leg sitting. Tender over greater trochanter. Previously called "bursitis" but primarily tendinopathy.Palpation over greater trochanter. FABER test ± positive. Single-leg squat provokes pain. Hip abductor weakness.Load management (avoid aggravating positions). Isometric gluteal exercises (pain-free muscle activation). Eccentric gluteal loading progression. Physiotherapy ★★★. Avoid: sleeping on affected side, pigeon stretch (compresses tendon), foam rolling over GT.
Femoroacetabular Impingement (FAI)Young active patients. Groin pain with deep hip flexion. Cam deformity (femoral head non-spherical). Pincer deformity (excessive acetabular coverage).FADIR test ★ (flexion 90° + adduction + IR → groin pain). Scour test. CT or MRI arthrography for definitive assessment. XR: pistol grip deformity (cam), crossover sign (pincer).Activity modification. Physiotherapy: hip strengthening + movement pattern retraining. Avoid: deep squats, hip flexion end-range. Intra-articular injection (corticosteroid) diagnostic + therapeutic. Surgery if conservative management fails >6 months.
Hip Flexor Tightness & Psoas SyndromeAnterior hip/groin pain. Often in: office workers, cyclists, runners. Thomas test positive. Lumbar hyperlordosis secondary to tight psoas.Thomas test: patient supine, draws one knee to chest — contralateral leg rises off table = hip flexor tightness. Modified Thomas: degree of hip extension measured.Hip flexor stretching (lunge stretch, Thomas stretch — 3×30sec, 3×/day). Yoga: Anjaneyasana, Bhujangasana, Supta Virasana (hero reclining). Foam rolling for iliopsoas and rectus femoris. Core stability strengthening (co-activation of psoas + deep abdominals).
🟡 Hip Yoga Protocol
Top 5 yoga postures for hip health: (1) Baddha Konasana (butterfly) — inner groin, adductors. (2) Eka Pada Raja Kapotasana (pigeon) — piriformis, external rotators — CONTRAINDICATED in FAI and GTPS (compressive load on tendon and acetabular rim). (3) Virasana (hero pose) — hip flexors, quads. (4) Anjaneyasana (crescent lunge) — psoas, hip flexors. (5) Supta Kapotasana (supine pigeon/figure 4) — external rotators, piriformis — safer alternative to pigeon for FAI. Always assess: does this patient have FAI or GTPS before prescribing pigeon pose — in these conditions it is specifically contraindicated.

cards.title: Sports Injury — POLICE Protocol & Rehabilitation Stages
cards._legacyHtml:
POLICE (replaces RICE)
Optimal Loading is now preferred over Rest — controlled movement promotes healing and prevents deconditioning.
LetterInterventionHow to apply
P — ProtectionLimit harmful movementSplint/brace only if needed. Avoid complete immobilisation.
OL — Optimal LoadingEarly controlled movement ★Pain-free ROM exercises within 24–48 hours. Promotes collagen alignment.
I — IceCryotherapy (first 48–72h)10–20 min, 3–4× daily. Reduces pain + spasm. Evidence mixed for swelling.
C — CompressionElastic bandagingReduces oedema. Check for neurovascular compromise distally.
E — ElevationAbove heart levelReduces hydrostatic pressure + oedema.
PhaseTimelineGoalNaturopathic support
Acute (inflammatory)0–72 hoursControl swelling, pain managementBromelain 500mg TID, arnica topical, turmeric, ice
Proliferative (repair)3 days – 3 weeksCollagen synthesis, restore ROMVitamin C 1–2g/day ★ (collagen synthesis), zinc, silica
Remodelling3 weeks – 12 monthsStrength, proprioception, return to sportProgressive loading, protein 1.6–2.2g/kg, omega-3, yoga
🟡 Bromelain for acute injury
Bromelain (pineapple enzyme) 500–1000mg TID between meals reduces post-traumatic oedema, pain, and bruising as effectively as diclofenac in RCTs. Must be taken away from food (otherwise acts as digestive enzyme, not anti-inflammatory). Combine with curcumin and quercetin for synergistic anti-inflammatory effect. Avoid if on anticoagulants (antiplatelet activity).

cards.title: Tendinopathy — Eccentric Loading & Nutrition
cards._legacyHtml:
Tendinopathy — The Continuum Model
Cook and Purdam's tendon continuum: Reactive tendinopathy → Tendon disrepair → Degenerative tendinopathy. Stage determines treatment. Reactive tendinopathy: acute load management + isometrics. Disrepair/degenerative: progressive loading is THE most evidence-based treatment. Complete rest = tendon atrophy → worse prognosis.
TendonClinical FeaturesExercise Protocol
Achilles tendinopathyPosterior heel pain, morning stiffness (post-sleep stiffness that warms up with activity — classic). Tender on palpation mid-body or insertion. Runners, jumping sports, sudden increase in load.Alfredson protocol ★★★: eccentric calf raises 3×15 reps with straight knee + 3×15 with bent knee, twice daily × 12 weeks. Load progression: start BW, add weight as pain allows. HEAVY slow resistance (Beyer RCT) — as effective as Alfredson, better tolerated.
Patella tendinopathyAnterior knee pain at patella inferior pole. Worse: jumping, landing, stairs, sitting with bent knee. Athletes (volleyball, basketball — "jumper's knee").Isometric quad contractions 4×45sec → progress to single leg press heavy slow resistance. Spanish squat (horizontal force vector). Decline squat programme ★★★. Avoid: stretching painful tendon, deep squat early stages.
Rotator cuff tendinopathyAnterior/lateral shoulder pain, worse overhead activity. Common in: overhead athletes, painters, elderly (degenerative). Painful arc 60–120°.Rotator cuff isometrics (pain-free angles). Progress to: external rotation resistance band, side-lying ER, empty can → full can, and Y-T-W-L scapular exercises. Thoracic mobility essential.
1
Nutritional Support for Tendon Healing Collagen peptides (Fortigel, TENDOFORTE) 10–15g + 50mg Vit C 30–60 min before loading exercise ★★★ — Shaw et al. RCT: doubles tendon collagen synthesis. Collagen peptides provide hydroxyproline and proline — building blocks loaded specifically into tendon when mechanical stimulus is applied within 1 hr of ingestion. Vitamin C 500mg/day ★★★ — essential cofactor for prolyl hydroxylase (crosslinks collagen triple helix). Without adequate Vit C, structurally weak collagen forms. Glycine 3–5g/day (rate-limiting amino acid for collagen — often deficient in muscle-protein-focused diets). Vitamin D 3000–4000 IU (VDR expressed in tendon cells — deficiency → slower tendon healing). Omega-3 3g/day (reduces neurogenic inflammation in tendinopathy).

cards.title: Musculoskeletal Assessment — Systematic Approach
cards._legacyHtml:
1
Look — Posture, alignment, swelling, muscle wasting, skin changes, deformity (varus/valgus, kyphosis, scoliosis). Compare sides.
2
Feel — Temperature (↑ = active inflammation), tenderness (specific points vs diffuse), crepitus, effusion (patellar tap, cross-fluctuation), muscle tone
3
Move — Active then passive ROM. Pain on active only = muscular. Pain on passive = articular/capsular. End-feel: soft (effusion), firm (capsular tightness), hard (bone-on-bone). Record degrees.
4
Special tests — Specific to joint and suspected diagnosis (see below). Assess neurovascular status: sensation, pulses, reflexes distal to lesion.
5
Function — Can they perform their ADLs? Job-specific tasks? Sport movements? Use outcome tools (DASH, KOOS, Oswestry, VAS).
🟡 GALS screening
Gait-Arms-Legs-Spine. A 2-minute systematic screen: watch patient walk, assess shoulder abduction + hand function, knee squat, spinal flexion. Misses nothing significant. Ask: "Do you have any pain or stiffness in your muscles, joints or back?" — If yes, positive GALS → full regional exam required.

cards.title: Inflammatory vs Mechanical Pain — Differentiation
cards._legacyHtml:
FeatureInflammatory / ArthriticMechanical
Morning stiffness>60 minutes ★<30 minutes
Rest painYes — worse with restNo — improved with rest
Activity effectImproves with movementWorsens with movement
DistributionPolyarticular, symmetrical (RA), or axial (AS)Monoarticular, asymmetric
Systemic featuresFatigue, fever, weight loss, rashUsually absent
Night painCommonAbsent (unless positional)
Labs↑ CRP/ESR, RF, ANA, HLA-B27Normal
Naturopathic approachAnti-inflammatory protocol: omega-3, curcumin, elimination diet trialStructural: yoga, massage, exercise rehab, posture

cards.title: Low Back Pain — Assessment, Red Flags & Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
LBP Red Flags — NEVER Miss These
95% of LBP is benign mechanical. 5% has a serious underlying cause (tumour, fracture, infection, cauda equina). Missing these is a catastrophic clinical failure. Every LBP patient needs red flag screening at first presentation.
Red FlagConcernAction
Age >50 + no prior LBP episodeMalignancy, compression fracture (osteoporosis)XR lumbar spine. If cancer history: urgent MRI.
Bilateral leg weakness/numbness + bladder/bowel changeCauda equina syndrome — SURGICAL EMERGENCY999/108 immediately. MRI within hours. Do NOT delay.
Night pain waking from sleep (not positional)Malignancy, infectionESR, CRP, XR. Urgent referral if inflammatory markers ↑
Fever + LBPDiscitis/osteomyelitis (especially in IV drug users, recent surgery, diabetes)Emergency referral. MRI + blood cultures. IV antibiotics.
Unexplained weight loss + LBPMalignancyUrgent oncology workup. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis.
History of cancerMetastatic disease (prostate, breast, lung, kidney, thyroid most common)Urgent bone scan or MRI. Do NOT treat as mechanical LBP.
Saddle anaesthesia (perineal numbness)Cauda equina — EMERGENCYEmergency surgical referral immediately.
1
Mechanical LBP AssessmentMovement screen: forward flexion, extension, lateral flexion, rotation — identify movement direction preference. SLR (straight leg raise): <60° + radiating pain = disc herniation with nerve root involvement. Slump test: radicular pattern. FABER/FADIR for hip-origin referred pain. Neurological exam: dermatomal sensation, myotomal power (L4: tibialis anterior, L5: extensor hallucis longus, S1: gastrocnemius). Palpation: paravertebral muscle spasm, trigger points, facet joint tenderness.
2
Naturopathic LBP ProtocolAcute (<6 weeks): POLICE (Protect, Optimal Loading, Ice/heat, Compression, Elevation). Maintain activity (rest worsens outcomes ★★★). Heat therapy (moist heat 20 min) ★★★. Willow bark 240mg salicin/day ★★★ (comparable to diclofenac in RCT). Topical arnica/capsaicin ★★. Subacute/Chronic (>6 weeks): Exercise therapy ★★★ (most evidence-based treatment — any form). Yoga ★★★ (multiple RCTs — equal to physiotherapy). Acupuncture ★★★. Manual therapy ★★. Mindfulness-based stress reduction ★★★ (chronic LBP — addresses central sensitisation). Magnesium 400mg (muscle spasm). Anti-inflammatory diet.

cards.title: Osteoporosis — Assessment & Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Osteoporosis — T-score ≤ −2.5 (DEXA)
T-score −1.0 to −2.5 = Osteopenia. T-score ≤ −2.5 = Osteoporosis. Risk of fragility fracture increases 2x per SD decrease in BMD. Wrist, spine, and hip are primary fracture sites.
Risk FactorModifiableNon-Modifiable
MajorLow calcium/Vit D, smoking, alcohol, corticosteroids, low body weight, inactivityAge, female sex, menopause, family history, prior fragility fracture
Secondary causesCoeliac disease, IBD, thyrotoxicosis, hyperparathyroidism, T1DM, prolonged PPI use, anti-epileptics, aromatase inhibitors
InterventionDoseEvidenceNotes
Calcium ★1200mg/day total (food + supplement)★★★★Split doses — max 500mg absorbed per dose. Food sources preferred (dairy, sardines, tofu, leafy greens)
Vitamin D3 ★2000–4000 IU/day. Target 25(OH)D >75 nmol/L★★★★Must combine with calcium — D3 alone has less effect on fracture
Vitamin K2 (MK-7)100–200mcg/day★★★ GoodDirects calcium to bone (osteocalcin carboxylation), away from arteries. Essential with high-dose Ca+D3
Magnesium300–400mg/day★★★ GoodCofactor for Vit D activation and bone matrix proteins
Strontium ranelatePrescriptive in some countries★★★★ StrongReduces fracture risk 35–40%. Not available OTC everywhere
Resistance training ★2–3x/week, all major muscle groups★★★★ StrongMost potent stimulus for bone formation — mechanical loading activates osteoblasts
Isoflavones (post-menopausal)80–90mg/day soy isoflavones★★★ GoodOestrogenic effect on bone — reduces resorption
Collagen peptides10g/day★★★ GoodImproves bone mineral density + muscle mass in postmenopausal women (RCT)
🟡 FRAX Score
Use FRAX calculator (shef.ac.uk/FRAX) for every patient with osteopenia or risk factors. FRAX calculates 10-year probability of major osteoporotic fracture and hip fracture — guides treatment decisions. Treatment threshold varies by country. In India: consider bisphosphonates if 10-year hip fracture risk >3% or major fracture risk >20%, alongside naturopathic protocol. Always check secondary causes before starting treatment — untreated coeliac disease or hyperparathyroidism makes osteoporosis treatment ineffective.

cards.title: Knee Conditions — Assessment & Naturopathic Management
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionKey featuresSpecial testManagement
Osteoarthritis (OA) ★Age >45, morning stiffness <30 min, medial joint line pain, crepitus, Heberden/Bouchard nodes (hand OA)Medial/lateral joint line tenderness. X-ray: JSN, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosisExercise ★ (most evidence), weight loss (1kg off = 4kg off knee), glucosamine sulphate 1500mg, collagen hydrolysate, pool walking, hydrotherapy
Patellofemoral pain (PFPS)Anterior knee pain, worse stairs/squatting/prolonged sitting, "movie sign"Clarke's test (patellar grind), J-sign with extensionVMO strengthening (terminal extension), hip abductor strength, patellar taping, reduce hill running
Meniscal tearRotational injury or age-related (degenerative). Medial/lateral joint line pain, locking, giving way.McMurray test, Thessaly test (94% sensitive)Acute: RICE + early ROM. MRI for surgery decision. Exercise rehab often comparable to surgery for degenerative tears (RCT evidence).
ACL tearPivot/valgus injury, pop, immediate swelling (haemarthrosis), instabilityLachman test ★ (most sensitive), anterior drawer, pivot shiftSurgery vs rehab shared decision (depends on activity level, instability). Proprioception rehab regardless.
Patellar tendinopathyInferior patella pain, worse load (jumping, squatting). Jumper's knee in athletes.Palpation inferior pole. Royal London Hospital test.Heavy slow resistance ★ (isometric loading for pain relief, then eccentric decline squat). 12-week programme. Avoid rest alone.
🟡 Glucosamine sulphate — what the evidence actually says
Crystalline glucosamine sulphate (prescription-grade: Dona, Rottapharm) has the best evidence — GAIT trial subgroup and multiple European RCTs show ↓ pain, ↓ JSN progression in moderate-severe OA. Glucosamine hydrochloride (most OTC products) has much weaker evidence. Dose: 1500mg once daily (better than divided doses). Takes 4–8 weeks for effect. Combine with chondroitin sulphate 1200mg for synergistic effect.

cards.title: Nutritional Support for Joints & Connective Tissue
cards._legacyHtml:
NutrientRoleDoseEvidence
Glucosamine sulphateProteoglycan synthesis, cartilage matrix1500mg/dayGrade B for knee OA (sulfate > hydrochloride)
Chondroitin sulphateCartilage hydration, anti-catabolic800–1200mg/dayGrade B OA; combined with glucosamine
Type II collagen (undenatured)Oral tolerance → ↓ autoimmune cartilage destruction40µg/day (UC-II)Grade B RA adjunct, OA
Omega-3 (EPA+DHA)↓ Leukotriene B4, ↓ TNF-α, ↓ IL-1β2–4g/day EPA+DHAGrade A RA ★, Grade B OA
Vitamin D3Muscle function, pain modulation, anti-inflammatory2000–4000 IU/dayGrade B musculoskeletal pain, falls prevention
Magnesium (glycinate/malate)Muscle relaxation, nerve conduction, ATP300–400mg/dayGrade B muscle cramps, fibromyalgia
Boswellia serrata5-LOX inhibition → ↓ leukotrienes, anti-inflammatory300–400mg 3× (65% boswellic acids)Grade A OA knee ★, IBD adjunct
🟡 Boswellia — fastest onset herbal anti-inflammatory
Boswellia works within 1–2 weeks (vs 4–8 weeks for omega-3). Mechanism: selectively inhibits 5-lipoxygenase (5-LOX) → ↓ leukotrienes (not prostaglandins like NSAIDs) → anti-inflammatory without GI ulceration risk. AKBA (acetyl-11-keto-β-boswellic acid) is the most active component. Aflapin (patented form) shows superior bioavailability. Pair with curcumin for additive effect via complementary pathways.

cards.title: Fibromyalgia — Comprehensive Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Fibromyalgia — Central Sensitisation Syndrome
Fibromyalgia is not a psychological diagnosis — it is a disorder of central pain processing. Neuroimaging shows genuine amplified pain signal processing in the central nervous system. The 2016 ACR criteria: Widespread Pain Index ≥7 + Symptom Severity Scale ≥5, for >3 months. No longer requires tender point count.
InterventionEvidenceProtocolNotes
Aerobic exercise★★★ Most consistent evidence — reduces pain 30%, fatigue 25%, improves function. Aquatic exercise ★★★ (heated pool — unloaded, gentle, joint-friendly)Start very low (5–10 min walk). Increase by 10% per week. Goal: 150 min moderate aerobic/week over 3–6 months. Never push through post-exertional pain flare.Graded exercise therapy: most important non-pharmacological intervention. Resistance training also effective ★★.
Sleep priority★★★ Poor sleep → central sensitisation → more pain. Treating insomnia improves pain in FM ★★★ (fibromyalgia is as much a sleep disorder as a pain disorder)CBT-I for insomnia (first-line). Magnesium glycinate 400mg + malate 600mg. Sleep hygiene. Low-dose amitriptyline 10–25mg (conventional — improves sleep architecture and reduces FM pain).Without treating sleep, FM is extremely difficult to manage. Always prioritise sleep assessment and treatment.
SAMe (S-adenosyl methionine)★★★ Multiple RCTs: SAMe 400–800mg/day reduces FM pain, fatigue, depression, morning stiffnessSAMe 200mg BD increasing to 400mg BD over 4 weeks. Takes 4–8 weeks for effect. Antidepressant + analgesic + methylation donor.Avoid in: bipolar disorder (can trigger mania). Store in cool, dark place (unstable molecule). Expensive but strong evidence in FM specifically.
Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR)★★★ Cochrane: MBSR reduces pain catastrophising, improves pain acceptance and QoL in fibromyalgia8-week MBSR programme ★★★. Yoga Nidra daily. Breathwork (reduces sympathetic amplification of pain). Pain Neuroscience Education (PNE) ★★★ — explaining central sensitisation to patients reduces pain and catastrophising.Pain Neuroscience Education is one of the most powerful FM interventions — patients who understand WHY they have pain AND that it is not dangerous respond much better to all other treatments.
